trade.summary: France and the Netherlands are major EU trading partners with deeply integrated supply chains. The Port of Rotterdam is a key entry point for French imports. France exports agri-food, chemicals, and machinery to the Netherlands. The Netherlands re-exports and provides logistics, financial, and technology services back to France.

diplomatic.summary: France and the Netherlands are EU founding members with close alignment on most policy issues. The Netherlands tends toward liberal, free-market positions within the EU while France leans toward industrial policy and sovereignty — this creates occasional friction but not fundamental disagreement. Both are NATO members with aligned security views.

military.summary: Both NATO allies with close defence cooperation. The Netherlands participates in the French-led European Intervention Initiative. Joint naval exercises and intelligence sharing. Dutch F-35 fleet complements French Rafale in NATO structures. Close cooperation in Eastern Europe and Baltic security.

financial.summary: Massive financial flows. Many French multinationals use Dutch holding structures for tax and regulatory reasons. Shell (Anglo-Dutch) and other Dutch multinationals are significant investors in France. Amsterdam has gained financial firms from Paris post-Brexit (primarily London, but Amsterdam is now closer).

energy.summary: Close energy ties through shared EU electricity and gas networks. The Netherlands was a major gas exporter (Groningen field) that has wound down; now both countries are transitioning to renewables. Cooperation in offshore wind (North Sea) and hydrogen.

scientific.summary: Extensive research collaboration through EU frameworks. Dutch universities and CNRS have numerous joint projects. Cooperation in life sciences (Leiden, Utrecht with French institutions), physics, and data science is particularly strong. ASML in Netherlands and French chip policy are areas of strategic coordination.
